assumptions: The question is assessed for consolidated financial statements because it refers to an exposure within the consolidated perimeter.
The intragroup dividend has already been declared and has created a recognised intercompany receivable/payable, ie a monetary item rather than a forecast transaction.
The facts do not state whether foreign exchange gains or losses on the receivable/payable survive consolidation; that is the key missing condition.
A foreign currency hedge is relevant only if the receivable/payable is exposed to a currency different from at least one relevant entity’s functional currency.

approaches.label: General exclusion of intragroup items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, only exposures with parties external to the reporting entity can generally be designated as hedged items. Ordinary intragroup assets, liabilities and transactions are therefore excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not document the dividend receivable hedge in consolidation unless it fits an IFRS 9 exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to external-party exposures, subject to narrow exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: An intragroup monetary item, such as a receivable/payable, may qualify as a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if its FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. This exception is tied to different functional currencies within the group.
conditions: The dividend receivable/payable is a monetary item
The hedge is only for foreign currency risk
FX gains or losses on the item are not fully eliminated on consolidation under IAS 21
The entities involved have different functional currencies
The hedge relationship meets IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: A hedge may be documented, but only for the residual FX exposure that survives consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary receivable/payable to be a hedged item in consolidation when FX effects are not fully eliminated.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness criteria for any qualifying hedge.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: This exception applies only to a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction whose foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. A recognised dividend receivable is not a forecast transaction.
approaches.practical_implication: This model is not the basis for hedging an already recorded intragroup dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 6.3.6 and B6.3.5 permit certain forecast intragroup FX exposures in consolidation only when they affect consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraph B6.3.6 links OCI recycling to the periods when the forecast intragroup transaction affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: A separate hedge model exists for the foreign currency risk of a net investment in a foreign operation, measured by reference to the parent’s functional currency and the foreign operation’s functional currency. It is relevant only if the hedge is framed as a hedge of net assets, not merely of the dividend receivable.
conditions: The designated exposure is an amount of net assets of a foreign operation included in consolidated financial statements
The hedged risk is the FX difference between the foreign operation’s functional currency and a parent entity’s functional currency
Only one qualifying hedge of the same net investment/risk is recognised in the ultimate parent’s consolidated financial statements
IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements are satisfied
approaches.practical_implication: Consider this only as an alternative hedge model if the economics relate to a foreign net investment rather than to the intercompany receivable itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a distinct hedge accounting model.
Paragraphs 10–15 explain eligible risk, amount hedged, group location of the hedging instrument, and documentation for net investment hedges.
